summary: Yegge introduces 'The Wasteland,' a federated network linking thousands of AI-powered coding environments ('Gas Towns') into a shared work marketplace built on Dolt, a SQL database with Git semantics. The system centers on a 'Wanted Board' where anyone can post tasks, and contributors earn multi-dimensional 'stamps' for completed work that accumulate into a portable, auditable professional reputation. The core design principle is that reputation derives solely from verified work attested by others, making it a credibility system antithetical to LinkedIn's self-reported model. Yegge frames it as both a coordination protocol for large-scale AI-assisted engineering and the embryo of a global work identity layer. The post is part product launch, part community call-to-arms, with Yegge rallying volunteers and crediting a small core team that built the system largely without VC funding.
key_points: The Wasteland federates Gas Towns via Dolt (Git-semantics SQL), enabling distributed work coordination using the battle-tested fork/merge/PR workflow without inventing new protocols.

Stamps are multi-dimensional attestations (quality, reliability, creativity, confidence, severity) anchored to specific completions, making reputation fully traceable and auditable through an append-only ledger.
A trust-level ladder (registered → contributor → maintainer) creates a natural apprenticeship path where only those who've earned stamps can issue them.
The 'yearbook rule'—you cannot stamp your own work—is the foundational design decision that distinguishes the system from self-reported credentials like LinkedIn.
Yegge sees the Wasteland evolving toward a universal portable professional identity, with GitHub contribution history pre-seeded as a bootstrap for character sheets.
money_quotes: The Wasteland is a way to link thousands of Gas Towns together in a trust network, to build stuff really, really fast. So fast that your biggest problem will be ideas.
Going from Claude Code to Gas Town elevates you from pair-programming into large-scale engineering leadership.
The whole system is designed around one principle: work is the only input, and reputation is the only output.
And there's a yearbook rule: you can't stamp your own work. Your reputation is what others write about you, not what you claim about yourself.
Nobody cares what you say about yourself. They care what the people who reviewed your work say about you.
It's the beginning of a résumé you never have to write — one that proves what you can do.
Claude Code seems to be slipping into the classic 'we're a product, not a platform' trap, and the thundering herd is going to route right around that, as soon as it's thermodynamically possible.
The Wasteland is now building itself.
key_insight: By anchoring professional reputation exclusively to auditable, peer-attested work outputs rather than self-reported credentials, the Wasteland attempts to build a trust layer for AI-assisted collaborative work that could replace the resume as a professional identity primitive.
